prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 29-year-old man.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had routine bloodwork and feel fine. I had a bad cold about 2 weeks ago that has resolved. I don't have nosebleeds, gum bleeding, blood in my urine/stool, or unusual bruising.
 - History: No blood thinners or new medications.
 - Vitals: BP 112/70, HR 74
 - Exam: No abnormal exam findings reported.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - Platelets: 128 x10^3/uL (150-400) LOW
 - Hemoglobin: 13.1 g/dL (12.0-16.0) normal
 - WBC: 6.8 x10^3/uL (4.5-11.0) normal
 - INR: 1.0 (0.9-1.1) normal
 
 I don't have health insurance and can't afford an ER visit.
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your platelet count is mildly low at 128, just below the normal lower limit of 150. Everything else — hemoglobin, white blood cells, and clotting (INR) — looks normal. Your vitals are healthy, you have no bleeding symptoms, and your physical exam was normal. A very common and well-known cause of a temporary mild drop in platelets is a recent viral infection, which fits perfectly with the bad cold you had about 2 weeks ago. Viral illnesses can temporarily suppress platelet production or increase their turnover. In most healthy young people, platelets bounce back to normal within a few weeks after the infection resolves. Since you're not bleeding, not bruising, and feel fine, this is not an emergency. However, it's worth having your doctor recheck a complete blood count in a few weeks to confirm the platelets are recovering. If you develop any unusual bleeding or bruising before then, seek care sooner.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 92%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your platelet count is mildly low at 128, just slightly below the normal range of 150. Everything else — hemoglobin, white blood cells, and clotting (INR) — looks perfectly normal. Importantly, you have no bleeding symptoms at all. A very common and well-known cause of a mild, temporary drop in platelets is a recent viral infection, which fits perfectly with your bad cold about 2 weeks ago. Viral illnesses can temporarily suppress platelet production or cause mild immune-mediated platelet destruction. In most healthy young people, platelets bounce back to normal within a few weeks after the infection resolves. A count of 128 is not low enough to cause spontaneous bleeding (that risk generally starts below 50 or lower). This is not an emergency. However, it would be reasonable to have your platelets rechecked in a few weeks to confirm they're recovering, which would support the post-viral explanation.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 92%

Viral illnesses can suppress platelet production or increase their turnover for a short period, and counts typically recover on their own within a few weeks. At 128, you are not at any meaningful risk of spontaneous bleeding (that concern generally arises below 50).
